prevalence.source: Dermatology epidemiology syntheses; Indian dermatology clinic data
prevalence.trend: stable; increasing recognition of adult and female acne

burden.dalyPerCapita: low mortality; high psychosocial burden and scarring risk

vulnerablePopulations: patients with limited access to dermatology care
epidemiology.diagnosticCriteria: Clinical diagnosis; grade severity and identify scarring; consider endocrine evaluation for severe/refractory or hyperandrogenism signs

publicHealthSystem.screeningCoverage: no screening; care through primary care and dermatology
publicHealthSystem.controlRate: Moderate; improves with adherence and access to topical/systemic regimens

regionalSpecificities.diagnosisCriteria: Clinical diagnosis; dermatology referral for severe nodulocystic acne or scarring
regionalSpecificities.treatmentProtocol: Topicals (benzoyl peroxide, retinoids), antibiotics when indicated, hormonal therapy for selected women, isotretinoin for severe cases with monitoring
regionalSpecificities.regionalChallenges: Over-the-counter steroid misuse in some settings, antibiotic overuse, uneven access to dermatologists outside metros
regionalSpecificities.strongPoints: Wide availability of affordable generics and established dermatology services in urban centers
